title: Hobnobs
subtitle: Oaty British biscuits that hold together in a cup of tea
excerpt: Oaty biscuits made with rolled oats, golden syrup, and butter. Dense and slightly coarse in texture, they hold together when dunked. These are the homemade version of the supermarket staple, properly oat-flavoured rather than sweet.
sourceNotes: Oat biscuits in this form have a long history in British baking, with similar recipes appearing in household guides from the early 20th century before the branded product was introduced. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), records oatmeal biscuit recipes of this character. The brand name Hobnob is trademarked; the recipe here is the generic oat biscuit of the same form, which predates the commercial product.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly; bake in two batches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in a tin for up to 5 days.

glossaryTerms.definition: The characteristic texture of an oat biscuit comes from using rolled oats alongside flour. The oats do not fully integrate into the dough; they remain as distinct pieces that give the biscuit its rough surface and short, crumbly bite. Using oat flour or fine oatmeal produces a smoother, less characteristic result.

content.text: depends on using rolled oats, not oat flour. Fine oatmeal produces a smoother biscuit but loses the rough, nubbed surface. The golden syrup gives the biscuit its specific sweetness and helps it hold together without additional egg.

Preheat the oven to 180°C conventional. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Combine the oats, flour, sugar, salt, and bicarbonate of soda in a large bowl. Melt the butter and golden syrup together in a small saucepan, stirring until combined. Pour into the dry ingredients and mix to a stiff, slightly crumbly dough. Roll into balls of about 20 g each, place on the trays with 4 cm between each, and flatten to 6 mm thick with your palm. Bake for 10 to 12 minutes until deep golden on the edges and just set in the centre. They will feel soft when they come out; they harden as they cool.
Leave on the tray for 5 minutes before transferring to a rack. The biscuits are fragile while hot. Transfer carefully.
